costs: Foregone equity gains that active intervention could have captured during the same window
Continued high segregation and SES achievement gaps persisting without correction (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06, F-live-international_comparison-03)
Risk of uncoordinated, crisis-driven local closures rather than planned consolidation, particularly harming rural settlement viability (F-live-school_network_planning-10)
content.equity_impact: Passive contraction reduces the absolute scale of the selective track over time but does so unevenly (concentrated in rural/low-density areas already showing minimal uptake), leaving urban segregation and Roma/ethnic segregation dynamics largely untouched; it is a scale-reduction mechanism, not an equity-targeting mechanism, and should be treated as the null baseline rather than a policy solution.
expected_benefits: Requires no new legislation, political capital, or administrative redesign
Provides a necessary evidence baseline for evaluating whether active interventions (T1-T4) outperform passive demographic effects alone
Avoids Polish-style abrupt-transition disruption since no discrete reform event occurs
content.goal: Establish the explicit no-new-policy counterfactual in which shrinking birth cohorts mechanically force network consolidation and shrink the selective track's absolute size, against which active interventions (T1-T4) are measured.

implementation_steps.action: Continue current legal, admissions, and capacity settings unchanged; monitor cohort-driven attrition of rural/peripheral 6/8-year provision as it occurs
Respond ad hoc to individual school viability crises (e.g., approve/deny class formation) as cohorts fall below statutory minimums, without a systematic consolidation plan
Track and publish the passive contraction trajectory as a counterfactual baseline against any future active reform proposal

mechanisms: Cohort decline is structurally embedded for 20+ years via both falling fertility and a shrinking pool of reproductive-age women, meaning the contraction pressure is not a short-term fluctuation but a durable structural trend (F-live-demography-01, F-live-demography-02).

Statutory minimum class-size rules (26 minimum, 34 maximum) create a mechanical threshold below which forming even a single viable gimnázium intake class becomes numerically untenable in shrinking localities, independent of any policy choice (F-live-demography-05).

Rural settlements in specific regions already show 10-30% child-population contraction with some settlements down to 50-70 school-age children total, showing the mechanism is already operating in the current system without new policy (F-live-demography-04).

Applications data already show an emerging shift (6-year applications +11%, 8-year applications -14% in 2026) and stagnant physical capacity despite oversubscription, indicating passive market/demographic adjustment is already underway within the unchanged legal structure (F-live-school_network_planning-02, F-live-school_network_planning-03).

Aging teacher workforce (50% age 50+, only 7.2% under 30) with rural/disadvantaged shortages compounds the passive contraction pressure on peripheral selective provision without requiring active policy (F-live-demography-09, F-live-implementation_planning-07).
risks: Passive contraction is geographically uneven: urban/Budapest selective capacity may persist largely intact while rural provision collapses, potentially worsening geographic equity even as aggregate track size shrinks (F-live-demography-03, F-live-school_network_planning-05)

Uncoordinated closures could trigger settlement-viability consequences and unplanned disruption similar in kind (though smaller in scale) to the Polish transition-cost pattern, absent any phased management (F-live-school_network_planning-10, F-live-international_comparison-07)

Roma segregation and church-sector selective growth are not addressed at all by passive demographic contraction and could persist or worsen even as overall track size shrinks (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07, F-live-polish_reform-08)
content.title: Demographically-driven passive contraction baseline (no new policy)
